In the end, I added it above following alphabetical order... but please feel free to ask me to make changes to the `require` ordering.
author: mbargiel

body: Avoids issues treating URL objects like an Axios proxy object. (Like `proxy.host` having a port).
author: mbargiel

body:
All this logic was merged into `setProxy`. This is the key to re-evaluating the environment variables on redirects.

Oh, ... and it was replaced with a single call to the [`proxy-from-env`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/proxy-from-env) library (a [single line](https://github.com/axios/axios/pull/4436/files?w=1#diff-586c04c24608b86a074073c5514cc195be5c4c87ddb56da233f6d998ada4159fR31)).
author: mbargiel

body:
To use the right protocol defined by environment variable-set proxies on redirect, `setProxy` now overrides the `options.protocol` when needed. That allows us to simplify this logic a bit, but requires moving the `options.agent` assignment to after we set the proxy.

By the way, is it me or is the `agent` only necessary when `maxRedirects` is set to 0? Unless I'm mistaken, `httpsFollow/httpFollow` use `agents.https`/`agents.http`.
author: mbargiel

body: I changed this because this was failing with an error in `follow-redirects`. This can be considered a breaking change if, previously, users could provide either `http` or `https` without a trailing colon. If you want to preserve the ability to not specify the `:`, let me know and I'll adjust `setProxy` to normalize it as needed, when `options.protocol` is overridden.
author: mbargiel

body: I (somewhat hackily) re-used the proxy as both a proxy and the redirected endpoint. That saved me from starting another server to handle the redirection and having to deal with that extra server in `afterEach`. It would be cleaner, but that's for a single test. Let me know what you prefer.
author: mbargiel

body:
This `headers.host` assignment was done two different ways:
- When initializing the request: https://github.com/axios/axios/pull/4436/files?w=1#diff-586c04c24608b86a074073c5514cc195be5c4c87ddb56da233f6d998ada4159fL216
- When following redirects: https://github.com/axios/axios/pull/4436/files?w=1#diff-586c04c24608b86a074073c5514cc195be5c4c87ddb56da233f6d998ada4159fL41

I moved this here because it's now possible to have different effective proxying on redirects when re-resolving the proxy from environment variables - ie set for `http_proxy` but not set for `https_proxy`, or skipping due to `no_proxy` for one call but not a redirect, etc.

body:
Added tests are excellent and valuable 🙌. 

At present I suggest we don't recommend auto-serialization of form-data as the default method of choice. Instead, let's move it to be after the manual section and say that we support it now. After we have more tests, and after it is well accepted by the community, let's adapt and recommend it by default. 
Currently, conversion of files in node will not work. This is solved by https://github.com/axios/axios/pull/4469 but tests and feedback are needed in this area. 

This Pr goes way beyond adding tests to FormData payload as the title suggest. It includes FormData documentation, a whole section of documentation about AbortController, adding import from "form-data" to "Node" only, and what seems like unrelated changes to scripts (`cross-env`). I guess it is obvious why this is less than ideal. 
While the contribution is great it would be even better if it will be separated to PRs that are scoped to a certain topic/domain.

body:
HTTP requests can be made via an HTTP proxy using the CONNECT method as well. Shouldn't this only create the `TlsSocket` in case the destination is using HTTPS, and otherwise return the original socket that was used to make the CONNECT request?

EDIT: Oh I see further down that this is only used when the target is HTTPS. So for an HTTP proxy with a target that's not using TLS, it assumes it's not gonna use the connect method, which is quite reasonable for an HTTP client.
author: Nevon

body: @Nevon exactly, CONNECT is only used if the destination uses TLS. I figured it would not make that much sense for non-TLS destinations because of the extra round trip.

author: github-advanced-security

body:
What about https over https? You still need a tunnel in that case because you need end-to-end encryption with the intended recipient.

Just for reference, here's how I fixed HTTP CONNECT support for secure HTTPS proxying. My solution   relies on the underlying agents (I mentioned it [here](https://github.com/axios/axios/issues/4318#issuecomment-1176692529) but I found it a bit too hacky and it required introducing an unmaintained dependency, which I wasn't comfortable with). 

You could also check out my fork directly for ideas : https://github.com/mbargiel/axios/compare/e6f9026d51d5e1386bc6935e563d95c375c45d02...feature/fix-proxy/http-connect
author: mbargiel

body: The nice thing with agents is that you don't have to worry about problems with follow-redirects: the tunnel is established in a lower abstraction layer, and once Axios has the socket, it's established properly to the end server. Redirects work over the tunnel.

body:
I assume that `data` is `undefined` in the scenario? Would it not be better to just check that then?

```
(typeof data === 'undefined' || data && data.length === 0)
```

Feels safer than making specific checks against method? I mean, that is the actual issue, right? If no content; Do not decode.
author: lejahmie

body:
Thanks for your reply, yes indeed this is the case.

However the problem is that `data` is always `undefined` for requests without a body and would also skip the all decompression on those. (e.g `GET` request without a body)
As far as I could comprehend, `data` is only set before hand if it is a `POST` request. (EDIT: or any request with a body)

The response stream is handled after this `data` variable is checked. If you would include this `undefined` check the unit tests 
 - should support gunzip error handling
 - should support transparent gunzip

will fail as they would not decompress the response anymore.

## Pull Request Overview

This PR adds the missing `handlers` property to the `AxiosInterceptorManager` interface to resolve testing issues where direct access to interceptor handlers was not available through the TypeScript definitions.

**Key Changes:**
- Introduces new type aliases (`AxiosInterceptorFulfilled`, `AxiosInterceptorRejected`) to reduce duplication
- Adds the `AxiosInterceptorHandler` interface to represent individual interceptor handlers

body:
## Pull Request Overview

This PR refactors `AxiosError` and `CanceledError` from constructor functions using prototypal inheritance to ES6 classes with native `extends` syntax. The main changes modernize the codebase to use class syntax while maintaining the same API surface.

Key changes:
- Converted `AxiosError` from a constructor function to an ES6 class that extends `Error`
- Converted `CanceledError` to an ES6 class extending `AxiosError`
- Added tests to verify both errors are recognized as native errors by Node.js

### Reviewed Changes

Copilot reviewed 4 out of 4 changed files in this pull request and generated 5 comments.

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| lib/core/AxiosError.js | Refactored from constructor function to ES6 class, moved static error code properties outside class definition with comment explaining parser limitation |
| lib/cancel/CanceledError.js | Converted to ES6 class extending AxiosError, removed utils import as inherits helper is no longer needed |
| test/specs/core/AxiosError.spec.js | Added tests for native error detection and static error code property usage |
| test/specs/cancel/CanceledError.spec.js | Added test for native error detection |
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The status assignment has changed behavior. Previously, it was `response.status ? response.status : null`, which would set status to `null` if response.status was falsy (e.g., 0). Now it directly assigns `response.status`, which could be `0` or other falsy values. This is a breaking change if status 0 was previously normalized to null. Consider restoring the ternary operator: `this.status = response.status ? response.status : null;`
```suggestion
            this.status = response.status ? response.status : null;
```

body:
Thanks for submitting this fix! Changing isCancel to check for `CanceledError<any>` makes sense. However, I think we can improve the typings further by making `isCancel` generic:

```suggestion
export function isCancel<T = any>(value: any): value is CanceledError<T>;
```

This allows `isCancel` to preserve the generic type parameter `T` from `CanceledError,` rather than hardcoding it to `any`.

body:
## Pull request overview

This PR fixes a packaging issue where the `main` field in package.json was pointing to an ESM file (`index.js`) instead of the CommonJS artifact after the project switched to `"type": "module"`. This caused compatibility issues for tools that don't parse the exports map and rely solely on the `main` field.

**Key changes:**
- Changed `main` field from `"index.js"` to `"./dist/node/axios.cjs"` to point to the CommonJS build
- Added `module` field pointing to `"./index.js"` for better compatibility with bundlers that don't support exports maps (e.g., webpack 4)
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
## Pull Request Overview

This pull request adds support for React Native blob objects to enable proper handling of file uploads in React Native environments. React Native uses a special "blob" object structure with a `uri` field that differs from standard web Blob objects.

Key changes:
- Added two new utility functions `isReactNativeBlob` and `isReactNative` for detecting React Native blob objects and environment
- Modified `toFormData` helper to handle React Native blobs by checking for React Native FormData and blob objects before processing

### Reviewed Changes

Copilot reviewed 2 out of 2 changed files in this pull request and generated no comments.

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| lib/utils.js | Added `isReactNativeBlob` and `isReactNative` utility functions to detect React Native blob objects and environment, exported them in the utils module |
| lib/helpers/toFormData.js | Added conditional logic to append React Native blob objects directly when in a React Native environment |
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
## Pull request overview

Copilot reviewed 2 out of 2 changed files in this pull request and generated 10 comments.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The JSDoc description states "Determine if environment is React Native" but the function actually checks if a FormData instance has a `getParts` method, which is specific to React Native's FormData implementation. This mismatch between the documentation and implementation can confuse developers. Update the JSDoc to accurately describe that this checks for React Native's FormData implementation, not the environment itself.
```suggestion
 * Determine if a FormData instance is using React Native's FormData implementation
 * React Native `FormData` has a non-standard `getParts()` method
 * 
 * @param {*} formData The FormData instance to test
 * 
 * @returns {boolean} True if the given FormData appears to be React Native's implementation (has `getParts`), otherwise false
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
Inconsistent parameter naming. The JSDoc specifies `@param {*} value` but the function uses `value` as the parameter name for `isReactNativeBlob`, which is consistent. However, `isReactNative` uses `formData` as the parameter name, while other similar type-checking functions typically use `val` or `thing` (see `isBlob`, `isFile`, `isStream`, `isFormData`). Consider using `val` as the parameter name for consistency with the rest of the codebase.
```suggestion
 * @param {*} val The value to test
 * 
 * @returns {boolean} True if environment is React Native, otherwise false
 */
const isReactNative = (val) => typeof val.getParts !== 'undefined';
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
Inconsistent parameter naming. The function uses `value` as the parameter name, while other similar type-checking functions in this file typically use `val` or `thing` (see `isBlob` at line 210, `isFile` at line 176, `isStream` at line 228). Consider using `val` as the parameter name for consistency with the codebase convention.
```suggestion
 * @param {*} val The value to test
 * 
 * @returns {boolean} True if value is a React Native Blob, otherwise false
 */
const isReactNativeBlob = (val) => {
  return val && typeof val.uri !== 'undefined'
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The function name `isReactNative` is misleading. It checks if a FormData object has a `getParts` method, which is a property of React Native's FormData implementation, not whether the environment is React Native. This name suggests it's an environment check rather than a FormData type check. Consider renaming to `isReactNativeFormData` or `hasGetParts` to better reflect what it actually validates.
```suggestion
 * Determine if a FormData value uses the React Native implementation.
 * React Native `FormData` has a non-standard `getParts()` method.
 *
 * @param {*} formData The FormData-like value to test
 *
 * @returns {boolean} True if the value is a React Native FormData, otherwise false
 */
const isReactNativeFormData = (formData) => !!formData && typeof formData.getParts === 'function';

/**
 * @deprecated Use {@link isReactNativeFormData} instead.
 *
 * Backwards-compatible alias for isReactNativeFormData.
 */
const isReactNative = isReactNativeFormData;

/**
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: JSDoc comments have inconsistent formatting with trailing whitespace. Lines 184 and 186 have trailing spaces at the end, while lines 196 and 198 also have trailing spaces. The codebase's JSDoc comments typically don't have trailing blank lines with spaces. Remove the trailing whitespace for consistency.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The new React Native blob detection functionality lacks test coverage. The test file `test/specs/utils/isX.spec.js` contains tests for similar type-checking functions like `isBlob`, `isFile`, `isFormData`, etc., but there are no tests for `isReactNativeBlob` and `isReactNative`. Consider adding tests that validate the behavior with various inputs including edge cases like objects with `uri: undefined`, null/undefined inputs, and objects without the expected properties.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The React Native blob handling in toFormData lacks test coverage. The test files in `test/specs/helpers/toFormData.spec.js` and `test/unit/helpers/toFormData.js` test various scenarios for FormData conversion, but there are no tests for the new React Native blob functionality. Consider adding tests that verify React Native blobs are properly appended to FormData when using React Native's FormData implementation.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The type checking logic is too permissive. The current implementation `value && typeof value.uri !== 'undefined'` will return true for any object with a `uri` property, even if the property value is `undefined` (e.g., `{uri: undefined}` would pass). This could lead to incorrect detection of React Native blobs. Consider using a more robust check that verifies the value is an object and that the `uri` property exists and has a meaningful value.
```suggestion
  return isObject(value) && typeof value.uri === 'string' && value.uri !== '';
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
Missing null/undefined check for the input parameter. If `formData` is `null` or `undefined`, the expression `typeof formData.getParts` will throw a TypeError. Following the pattern used in other type-checking functions in this file (e.g., `isObject`, `isStream`), add a null/undefined check before accessing the property.
```suggestion
const isReactNative = (formData) => formData && typeof formData.getParts !== 'undefined';
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: Missing semicolons at the end of function declarations. While JavaScript allows omitting semicolons due to automatic semicolon insertion, the codebase consistently uses semicolons for all other similar function declarations (see lines 176, 210, 219, etc.). Add semicolons after the closing braces on lines 191 and 201 to maintain consistency.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
<!-- metadata:{"confidence":8,"steps":[{"text":"Looking at the code change, I need to analyze the condition placement and logic for handling React Native blobs. Let me examine the utility functions being used.","toolCalls":[{"toolName":"bash","input":{"command":"rg -n isReactNative lib/utils.js -A 5"}},{"toolName":"bash","input":{"command":"rg -n isReactNativeBlob lib/utils.js -A 5"}}]},{"text":"","toolCalls":[{"toolName":"think","input":{"thought":"Now I understand the utility functions:

1. `isReactNativeBlob(value)` - returns true if value has a `uri` property
2. `isReactNative(formData)` - returns true if formData has `getParts` method (indicating React Native's FormData)

Looking at the diff again:
```javascript
} else if (utils.isReactNative(formData) && utils.isReactNativeBlob(value)) {
  formData.append(renderKey(path, key, dots), convertValue(value));
  return false;
}
```

This is placed inside the block:
```javascript
if (value && !path && typeof value === 'object') {
```

The issue is the `!path` condition. When `path` is truthy (an array for nested properties), this entire block is skipped. So nested React Native blobs won't be caught by this new condition.

For nested blobs, the code would fall through to:
```javascript
if (isVisitable(value)) {
  return true;
}
```

Since `isVisitable` checks for plain objects (`utils.isPlainObject(thing) || utils.isArray(thing)`), and a React Native blob like `{ uri: 'file://...' }` is a plain object, it would return `true`, causing unwanted recursion into the blob's properties.

This is a legitimate bug in the implementation - nested React Native blobs won't work correctly.

However, looking at existing patterns in the codebase - other special handling like arrays and file lists are also only at the root level. But those cases make sense because arrays get flattened differently at root.

For React Native blobs though, they should be handled at any level. The blob should be appended directly regardless of nesting depth.

Actually, wait - let me look at the condition more carefully. The check `!path` means this only runs when `path` is falsy. Looking at how the code works:

1. First call: `build(obj)` - path is undefined
2. In forEach, visitor is called with path (undefined at first level)
3. For nested: `build(el, path ? path.concat(key) : [key])` - path becomes an array

So at the first level of properties, `path` is `undefined`, making `!path` true. For nested properties, `path` is an array like `['nestedKey']`, making `!path` false.

This means the React Native blob handling only works at the first nesting level, not for deeply nested blobs.

This is a real bug, but let me consider severity:
- It's a new feature, so the bug is in new functionality
- Nested blobs won't be handled - they'll be recursed into incorrectly
- This could cause data loss or malformed form data

However, I should also check if this is really a bug or intentional. Looking at the PR description, it says it's adding support for React Native blobs that have a `uri` field. The React Native FormData can detect and upload them directly from filesystem.

The bug is real: the condition placement inside `!path` block limits the functionality to only root-level blobs, not nested ones. The fix would be to either:
1. Move the condition outside the `if (value && !path && typeof value === 'object')` block, or
2. Add a similar check after the block

Actually, looking more carefully at the logic flow - after the big if block, there's:
```javascript
if (isVisitable(value)) {
  return true;
}

formData.append(renderKey(path, key, dots), convertValue(value));
```

For non-root level React Native blobs:
1. The `if (value && !path && typeof value === 'object')` block is skipped
2. `isVisitable({ uri: '...' })` returns true (it's a plain object)
3. So it returns true, causing recursion into the blob object
4. This would try to append the `uri` string property instead of the blob object

This is definitely a bug. The React Native blob check should happen before the `isVisitable` check, regardless of the path level.

Let me format this as a violation."}}]},{"text":"Now I understand the issue. Let me verify the `isVisitable` function and confirm the bug:","toolCalls":[{"toolName":"bash","input":{"command":"rg -n isPlainObject lib/utils.js -A 8 | head -30"}}]},{"text":"","toolCalls":[{"toolName":"bash","input":{"command":"rg -n isPlainObject lib/utils.js -B 2 -A 10"}}]}]} -->
P1: React Native blob check is inside the `!path` condition, so it only works for root-level properties. Nested blobs (e.g., `{ nested: { file: { uri: '...' } } }`) will be recursed into instead of being appended directly, since `isVisitable` returns true for plain objects. Consider moving this check outside the `if (value && !path && typeof value === 'object')` block, or adding a similar check before the `isVisitable` check.

<details>
<summary>Prompt for AI agents</summary>

body:
## Pull Request Overview

This PR attempts to address issue #5888 by adding fallback values for potentially undefined zlib constants. However, the implementation has several significant issues.

**Key Changes:**
- Adds fallback value `|| 2` to `BROTLI_OPERATION_FLUSH` constant references in `brotliOptions` object

**Issues Identified:**
1. **Mismatch between description and implementation**: The PR description describes fixing `Z_SYNC_FLUSH` in `zlibOptions`, but the code only modifies `BROTLI_OPERATION_FLUSH` in `brotliOptions`
2. **Questionable approach**: Using hardcoded fallback values masks underlying environmental issues rather than addressing root causes
3. **Redundant with existing checks**: The code already has `isBrotliSupported` checks that guard Brotli usage
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The hardcoded fallback value of `2` may not be the correct value for `BROTLI_OPERATION_FLUSH`. In Node.js's zlib module, `BROTLI_OPERATION_FLUSH` has a value of `2`, but relying on a hardcoded value is fragile and could break if the constant's value changes or differs across Node.js versions.

More importantly, if `zlib.constants.BROTLI_OPERATION_FLUSH` is undefined, it indicates that either:
1. The Node.js version doesn't support Brotli (added in Node.js 10.16.0)
2. There's a deeper environmental issue with the zlib module

The code already has a check for Brotli support on line 38 (`isBrotliSupported`). A better approach would be to either:
- Rely on that existing check to prevent using brotli when it's not supported
- Guard the constant access with a proper check rather than using a fallback

Consider removing the `|| 2` fallback or implementing a more robust check for Brotli support.

## Pull Request Overview

This PR adds support for handling JavaScript `Set` objects in the `toJSONObject` utility method, converting them to arrays in the JSON output. This addresses issue #5910 where Set objects were not being properly serialized.

**Key Changes:**
- Added `isSet` helper function to detect Set objects
- Enhanced `forEach` utility to iterate over Set objects by converting them to arrays
- Updated `toJSONObject` to treat Sets as array-like structures in JSON conversion
- Added test coverage for nested Set handling with circular reference protection

### Reviewed Changes

Copilot reviewed 2 out of 2 changed files in this pull request and generated 4 comments.

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| lib/utils.js | Implemented Set detection and handling logic in core utilities |
| test/unit/utils/utils.js | Added test case verifying Set-to-array conversion with nested Sets |


<details>
<summary>Comments suppressed due to low confidence (1)</summary>

**lib/utils.js:292**
* Avoid automated semicolon insertion (92% of all statements in [the enclosing function](1) have an explicit semicolon).
```
    const arr = [...obj]
```
</details>
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
Missing semicolon at the end of the arrow function definition. All other similar function definitions in this file use semicolons (e.g., line 203).
```suggestion
const isSet = val => val instanceof Set;
```

body:
Missing semicolon at the end of the statement. This is inconsistent with the coding style used throughout the file where semicolons are used consistently.


body:
The `forEach` callback for Sets passes the converted array as the third parameter instead of the original Set object. This is inconsistent with the documented behavior where "If 'obj' is an Object callback will be called passing the value, key, and complete object for each property." Consider passing the original Set object: `fn.call(null, arr[i], i, obj);` to maintain API consistency.
```suggestion
      fn.call(null, arr[i], i, obj);
```

body: The `isSet` function should use `kindOfTest('Set')` instead of `instanceof Set` to be consistent with similar type-checking functions in this module. Most built-in types like `Date`, `File`, `Blob`, `ArrayBuffer`, and `URLSearchParams` use `kindOfTest`, which provides more robust type checking by examining the internal [[Class]] property. This approach is more reliable, especially when dealing with objects from different JavaScript contexts (e.g., iframes).
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The new `isSet` function lacks unit tests. Similar type-checking functions in this module like `isStream`, `isBuffer`, and `isFormData` have dedicated test cases. A test case should be added to verify that `isSet` correctly identifies Set instances and returns false for non-Set values.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The JSDoc comment for the `isSet` function uses an arrow function syntax in the function definition, but the JSDoc should describe it like other similar functions in the module. The actual function definition should also be consistent - either use `const isSet = (val) => ...` with parentheses around the parameter (like `isStream` on line 203) or use a full function declaration (like `isBuffer`). Most single-parameter arrow functions in this module use parentheses for consistency.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The comment "Convert Set to Array" is slightly misleading. The spread operator creates a new array from the Set's values, but doesn't convert the Set itself. Consider rephrasing to "Create array from Set values" or "Convert Set values to array" for clarity.
```suggestion
    // Create array from Set values
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The callback is being passed `arr` (the converted array) as the third argument instead of the original `obj` (the Set). This is inconsistent with how Arrays are handled on line 288, where the original array `obj` is passed as the third argument. For consistency and to match the documented behavior, the third argument should be the original Set object, not the converted array.
```suggestion
      fn.call(null, arr[i], i, obj);
```

body:
## Pull Request Overview

This PR addresses compatibility with frozen prototypes (Hardened JS/SES environments) by replacing direct property assignments with `Object.defineProperty()` calls to avoid the JavaScript "override mistake" that causes failures when prototypes are frozen.

**Key Changes:**
- Modified `extend()` to use `Object.defineProperty()` instead of direct assignment
- Updated `inherits()` to define the constructor property using `Object.defineProperty()`
- Refactored `reduceDescriptors()` to use spread operator instead of direct assignment
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The property descriptors are missing critical attributes. When using `Object.defineProperty()`, unspecified attributes default to `false`, which differs from direct assignment behavior where they default to `true`. This means properties will not be writable, enumerable, or configurable.

The descriptor should be:
```javascript
Object.defineProperty(a, key, {
  value: bind(val, thisArg),
  writable: true,
  enumerable: true,
  configurable: true
});
```

This applies to both branches of the conditional to maintain compatibility with the original behavior of direct property assignment.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The `constructor` property descriptor is missing critical attributes. When using `Object.defineProperty()`, unspecified attributes default to `false`, which differs from direct assignment behavior. The `constructor` property should be writable and configurable to match standard JavaScript prototype behavior.

The descriptor should be:
```javascript
Object.defineProperty(constructor.prototype, 'constructor', {
  value: constructor,
  writable: true,
  enumerable: false,
  configurable: true
});
```

Note: `enumerable: false` is correct for constructor properties (this matches the standard behavior), but `writable` and `configurable` should be `true`.
```suggestion
    value: constructor,
    writable: true,
    enumerable: false,
    configurable: true
```

body:
This change breaks the `reduceDescriptors` function. The `reducedDescriptors` object is used as the second argument to `Object.defineProperties(obj, reducedDescriptors)` on line 604, which expects an object where each property value is a property descriptor object (with keys like `value`, `writable`, `get`, `set`, etc.).

By wrapping the descriptor in `Object.defineProperty`, you're creating a data property where `reducedDescriptors[name].value` equals the descriptor, instead of `reducedDescriptors[name]` being the descriptor itself.

The original assignment `reducedDescriptors[name] = ret || descriptor;` should be kept as-is. The `reducedDescriptors` object is a plain object being built up, not a frozen/sealed prototype, so the original assignment will work correctly.
```suggestion
      reducedDescriptors[name] = ret || descriptor;
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
## Pull Request Overview

This PR attempts to fix issue #4537 by changing how errors are handled in interceptors - making the `onRejected` handler execute in the same interceptor where the error occurs, rather than in the next interceptor. The PR also addresses a TypeError that occurred when config was lost during the promise chain.

Key changes:
- Modified asynchronous request interceptor chain execution to use `.then().catch()` pattern with config preservation logic
- Modified response interceptor chain to use `.then().catch()` pattern instead of `.then(fulfilled, rejected)`
- Added three test cases to verify error handling behavior in interceptors

### Reviewed Changes

Copilot reviewed 2 out of 2 changed files in this pull request and generated 7 comments.

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| lib/core/Axios.js | Modified promise chain logic for both request and response interceptors to handle errors in the same interceptor, including addition of config preservation mechanism |
| test/specs/interceptors.spec.js | Added three new test cases covering error handling in request/response interceptors and config modification behavior when errors occur |
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The test logic is flawed. The test expects both `rejectedSpy1` and `rejectedSpy2` to be called, but based on the interceptor execution order (reversed for request interceptors), the second interceptor (with `rejectedSpy2`) will execute first and throw `error2`. If the error is handled in the same interceptor as intended by this PR, `rejectedSpy2` should be called with `error2`, but the first interceptor (with `rejectedSpy1`) should never execute because the promise chain would be rejected.

The test needs to clarify the expected behavior: should the request continue after an error is handled, or should it stop? The current assertions don't match either scenario correctly.
```suggestion
      expect(rejectedSpy1).not.toHaveBeenCalled();
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
Changing from `promise.then(fulfilled, rejected)` to `promise.then(fulfilled).catch(rejected)` changes the error handling semantics. In the original pattern, if `rejected` handler throws an error, it propagates immediately. In the new pattern, the error from `rejected` is caught by a subsequent `.catch()` in the next iteration.

However, unlike the request interceptor change, this change doesn't have the problematic `prevResult` logic, so it might actually work as intended for making the error handler part of the same interceptor. But this needs to be consistent with the request interceptor implementation, and both should be carefully reviewed for correctness.
```suggestion
      promise = promise.then(responseInterceptorChain[i++], responseInterceptorChain[i++]);
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
Inconsistent spacing around braces. The arrow function should have a space before the opening brace for consistency with the project's style:

```javascript
.then(result => { prevResult = result ? result : prevResult })
```

However, this entire line should be removed as part of fixing the flawed promise chain logic.
```suggestion

```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The promise chain logic has several critical issues:

1. **Falsy value handling**: Line 166 uses `result ? result : prevResult`, which will incorrectly fall back to `prevResult` for valid falsy values like `0`, `false`, `""`, or `null`. This should use `result !== undefined ? result : prevResult`.

2. **Lost error context**: The `.catch()` on line 167 consumes the error but then line 168 returns `prevResult`, which loses the error entirely. If an interceptor's `onRejected` handler throws or doesn't handle the error properly, the chain should propagate the error, not silently continue with the previous config.

3. **Incorrect chain behavior**: This implementation breaks the standard promise chain flow. After a `.catch()` returns successfully, the next `.then()` should receive that return value, not the `prevResult`.

The original implementation `promise.then(chain[i++], chain[i++])` properly passes both fulfilled and rejected values through the chain. The new implementation fundamentally changes this behavior in a way that breaks error propagation.

However, it points out a good question. Both patterns cannot handle the errors in rejected handler elegantly. I think we can leave this unchanged.

author: Tackoil

body:
After consideration,  I think we should just filter out some empty return values here.

According to the [docs](https://axios-http.com/docs/interceptors), every interceptor has a return value. For request interceptor, the return value should be a `config`. It can be identified by some specific properties. However, for response interceptors, the return value is user-defined. We cannot verify the result.

Therefore, I propose to choose a simple approach: filter out any return values that are `undefined`.

Instead of getting a confusing `Buffer.from()` error when both username and password are empty strings, users now receive an `AxiosError` with the message "Invalid proxy authorization" and code "ERR_BAD_OPTION".

body:
**[ReviewBot AI] qs library upgrade addresses prototype pollution**

The qs library was upgraded from 6.11.0 to 6.13.0, likely addressing known prototype pollution vulnerabilities.

**Suggested fix:**
```
Ensure application code does not rely on deprecated qs parsing behaviors. Review any custom query string parsing logic.
```

The qs library has had multiple CVEs related to prototype pollution (CWE-1321). Version 6.13.0 includes security patches. Verify that the upgrade doesn't break existing query parameter parsing in your application, especially if you use nested objects or arrays in query strings.

> CWE-1321

---

**[ReviewBot AI] Express upgraded from 4.18.2 to 4.21.1**

Express version jump indicates potential security fixes for routing, middleware, or request handling vulnerabilities.

**Suggested fix:**
```
Test all Express routes, middleware chains, and error handlers after upgrade. Verify cookie handling and body parsing behavior.
```

Express 4.21.1 includes security patches for body-parser, cookie handling, and other core components. The upgrade also brings updated dependencies like finalhandler and send which have had security issues. Thoroughly test authentication flows and file serving endpoints.

> CWE-20

---

**[ReviewBot AI] body-parser upgraded from 1.20.2 to 1.20.3**

Body-parser upgrade may address request body parsing vulnerabilities or denial of service issues.

**Suggested fix:**
```
Test JSON, URL-encoded, and multipart form data parsing. Verify request size limits are still enforced.
```

Body-parser versions have addressed content-type confusion and DoS vulnerabilities. Ensure your application properly validates request bodies and enforces size limits to prevent memory exhaustion attacks.

> CWE-400

---

**[ReviewBot AI] call-bind dependency significantly updated to 1.0.7**

Major version jump in call-bind adds new dependencies like es-errors and set-function-length, increasing dependency tree complexity.

**Suggested fix:**
```
Review transitive dependencies: es-define-property, es-errors, set-function-length. Ensure no breaking changes affect polyfills.
```

While this is a dev dependency, the expanded dependency tree (5 new transitive dependencies) increases attack surface. Audit these new packages for supply chain risks and ensure they don't conflict with existing polyfills or runtime behaviors.

body:
The function call has incorrect parameter order. The second parameter should be the config object, but here headers are passed as a third parameter. This should be: `http2Axios.get('', { baseURL: 'https://' + user + '@127.0.0.1:4444/', headers })`
```suggestion
    const res = await http2Axios.get('', { baseURL: 'https://' + user + '@127.0.0.1:4444/', headers });
```

## Pull Request Overview

This PR introduces an Undici adapter to add support for high-performance HTTP requests using Undici, with fallbacks for features not supported by undici.request.  
- Added a new adapter in lib/adapters/undici.js with upload/download progress support and proper error handling.  
- Updated tests in test/unit/adapters/undici.js to cover various response types, progress events, and abort scenarios.  
- Modified configuration files (package.json, lib/defaults/index.js, lib/adapters/adapters.js, and index.d.cts) to integrate and expose the Undici adapter.

Copilot reviewed 9 out of 9 changed files in this pull request and generated 1 comment.

## Pull Request Overview

This PR aligns the CommonJS type definitions in `index.d.cts` with the ESM definitions by updating signatures and adding missing methods.

- Broaden `AxiosHeaderMatcher` to accept `string` or `RegExp`
- Add `getSetCookie` on `AxiosHeaders` and a `static from` factory on `AxiosError`
- Fix `InternalAxiosRequestConfig` generic parameter and expose `mergeConfig`

**index.d.cts:9**
* The new `AxiosHeaderMatcher` signature omits the `headers: RawAxiosHeaders` parameter from the callback, which may break existing matchers that rely on access to all headers. Consider reintroducing that parameter or updating all downstream usage accordingly.
```
type AxiosHeaderMatcher = string | RegExp | ((this: AxiosHeaders, value: string, name: string) => boolean);
```
**index.d.cts:80**
* Missing JSDoc comment for `getSetCookie`. Add a description explaining what the returned strings represent (e.g., parsed `Set-Cookie` header values) and any edge-case behavior.

```

```

```

```

body:
This is a type-breaking change. Previously, you guaranteed that if `cause` is not-null, that it would be an Error, allowing users to do `axiosError.cause?.message`.

This change makes this code throw a type error when upgrading:

```
src/services/backendClient.ts:133:70 - error ts(2339): Property 'message' does not exist on type '{}'.

133                 detail: `Failed to make request: ${axiosError.cause?.message}`,
                                                                         ~~~~~~~
```

It seems that the LLM-generated PR-review message didn't catch that at all and was misleading:

> Types: Ensures `AxiosError.cause?: unknown` and `event?: ProgressEvent` are present in type definitions.
> Type declarations updated to include safe optional fields: `cause?: unknown` and `event?: ProgressEvent`.
>
> ### 🧵 Any breaking changes?
> No breaking API changes. This preserves backwards-compatible optional behaviors; adapters will still function normally if no err.code or event is present.

This is in fact a non-backwards compatible _change_.
author: corneliusroemer

## Pull Request Overview This PR modernizes axios's cookie handling by replacing the synchronous `document.cookie` API with the asynchronous `cookieStore` API to eliminate jank issues. The implementation includes fallback support for browsers without cookieStore support. - Converts cookie operations (read, write, remove) from synchronous to asynchronous - Updates resolveConfig function to handle async cookie reading for XSRF tokens - Modifies XHR and fetch adapters to properly handle the async configuration resolution ### Reviewed Changes Copilot reviewed 6 out of 6 changed files in this pull request and generated 2 comments.
Show a summary per file | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | lib/helpers/cookies.js | Implements cookieStore API with document.cookie fallback | | lib/helpers/resolveConfig.js | Updates XSRF token reading to be async | | lib/adapters/xhr.js | Wraps resolveConfig call in Promise chain | | lib/adapters/fetch.js | Adds await to resolveConfig call | | test/specs/helpers/cookies.spec.js | Updates tests to handle async cookie operations | | test/specs/requests.spec.js | Modifies timeout tests and adds cookieStore mocking |
--- **Tip:** Customize your code reviews with copilot-instructions.md. Create the file or learn how to get started.

The CookieStore API expects `expires` to be a number (timestamp in milliseconds since epoch), not a UTC string. When `expires` is a number, it should be passed directly without conversion to `toUTCString()`. The correct format should be just assigning the timestamp value.
```suggestion
            cookieOptions.expires = expires;
```

body:
The expires handling logic may not work correctly. The code checks if `expires` is a number, but then sets `cookieOptions.expires` to a string using `toUTCString()`. However, the Cookie Store API's `set()` method expects `expires` to be a `DOMTimeStamp` (number of milliseconds since epoch), not a string. The value should be passed as-is if it's already a number, rather than being converted to a string.
```suggestion
            cookieOptions.expires = expires;
```

## Pull Request Overview This PR introduces experimental HTTP/2 support for Axios by adding a new `http2` adapter alongside the existing HTTP adapters. body: It's unclear why you're regressing the code to use var, using arrays instead of a hash object, and matching for both regular expressions at once. The only purpose of `parseHeaders` is to parse headers into an object representation. There shouldn't be any high-level manipulations that could lose or corrupt the original headers. For high-level header manipulations, the `AxiosHeaders` class was introduced. Similar logic can be written in `AxiosHeaders.getAttachment()` method, but definitely not in the low-level parseHeaders utility. This way, the user will be able to operate with the original headers and obtain the decoded file name if needed.

body:
Thanks @neel3o115, and apologies on the wait. The diagnosis is right, Android's RN networking layer rejects bare `multipart/form-data` without a boundary, iOS doesn't, and the gap exists because RN is neither `hasStandardBrowserEnv` (axios deliberately excludes RN from that flag in `resolveConfig`) nor uses Node's `form-data` package. The placeholder header reaches the wire untouched on RN, and Android complains. Worth fixing.

Three asks before this can merge.

First, drop the `toFormData.js` changes. `Function('return this')()` is `eval` in a different shape — it trips CSP without `unsafe-eval`, which covers most production browsers behind a CSP and several RN setups. `globalThis` is universally available everywhere axios runs if global access were ever needed, but it isn't here. The precedence flip from `PlatformFormData || FormData` to `runtimeFD || PlatformFormData` is also a silent breakage on Node: Node 18+ has global `FormData`, so this picks the spec-compliant one over `form-data`, and any caller of `axios.toFormData(obj)` on Node who relied on `.getHeaders()` / `.getLength()` (documented form-data API) gets an object without them. And it isn't needed for the RN bug — RN bundlers resolve the `browser` field in `package.json`, so `PlatformFormData` already points at `lib/platform/browser/classes/FormData.js`, which is `typeof FormData !== 'undefined' ? FormData : null`. In RN, that's the runtime's `FormData`.

Second, tighten the `Axios.js` change. What you have:

```js
  headers: isForm
    ? (config && config.headers ? config.headers : {})
    : {},
````

`config.headers` is already merged in by `mergeConfig(config || {}, …)` — the second argument is the overlay, not the source. Re-passing config.headers is either a no-op or runs the header merge strategy twice. The minimal version is `headers: {}`, or drop the headers key from the overlay entirely. Same effect: no placeholder is forced, anything in config.headers still flows through normally.

Third, add tests. At minimum: `postForm` (and put/patch) no longer emit the bare placeholder; Node form-data path still ends up with `Content-Type: multipart/form-data; boundary=…` via `getHeaders();` spec-compliant FormData path through the http adapter still gets a boundary via `formDataToStream`.

## Pull request overview

This PR prevents a module-load `TypeError` in the fetch adapter by making destructuring from `utils.global` safe when it is `undefined` (as reported in #7259).

**Changes:**
- Add a default value (`= {}`) to the destructured parameter used to extract `Request`/`Response` from `utils.global`.
- Guard `ReadableStream`/`TextEncoder` destructuring by falling back to `{}` when `utils.global` is falsy.

body:
Thanks @Jye10032. When fetch.js's top-level code runs before `utils.js`'s default export has finished populating (a circular-import / evaluation-order quirk that bundlers like Vite can surface), `utils.global` reads as `undefined`, and the original destructure has no default, so it throws at module load before anything in axios can recover. Moving the read into `factory()` sidesteps that entirely; by the time `factory` is invoked from `getFetch()`, the ESM graph has settled.

Two small simplifications before this lands.

Flatten the fallback. `utils.global` is itself `globalThis ?? self ?? window ?? global`, so the only realistic case where it's undefined is the circular-import one and in that case `globalThis` is equally available. The `?? {}` arm is unreachable in any environment that runs ESM:

```js
const globalObject = utils.global ?? globalThis;
````

Drop the IIFE around Request/Response. With globalObject already in scope, the IIFE's only purpose (handling destructure-of-undefined at module load) is gone:

```js
const { Request, Response } = globalObject;
const globalFetchAPI = { Request, Response };
```

Same shape going into the `utils.merge.call(…, globalFetchAPI, env)` below.

Optional but nice: a regression test that stubs `utils.global` to undefined, calls `factory({})`, and asserts no throw. The bundler-graph case is hard to reproduce in unit tests directly, but stubbing the module is a reasonable proxy.

author: cubic-dev-ai

body:
Thanks for the patch. Quick flag: this does more than the title says, and I think the framing is getting in the way of landing it.

1. `onRejected.call(this, error)` runs unconditionally. If you do `interceptors.request.use(fn)` without a rejected handler, the current code does `undefined.call(...)` and you get a TypeError that buries your actual error. Your if (onRejected) guard fixes that.
2. After break, dispatchRequest runs with the previous newConfig and the request goes out anyway. The thrown error is silently swallowed. The added throw error is what makes synchronous mode behave like people expect.

Two semantic things worth thinking through:

The async chain supports recovery if `onRejected` returns a value, the chain continues with that value. Your sync version discards the return value and always rethrows. That means you can't recover from an error in sync mode, but you can in async mode. Is that a problem? If so, the sync version should return the value of `onRejected.call(this, error)` instead of throwing.

If onRejected itself throws, onRejected's error wins (the outer throw error never runs). That matches the async chain, but a test that pins the contract would be nice.

Smaller stuff:

- The new test file is at test/unit/core/interceptors.sync.error.spec.js. This repo uses tests/unit/core/*.test.js — pluralized tests, no .spec.js. Worth checking whether vitest is picking it up at the path you've used; if not, that explains why CI is green for the wrong reason.
- The diff adds a stray space to the } catch (error) { line.

```text
<violation number="1" location=".github/workflows/update-sponsor-block.yml:43">
P2: The "Exit gracefully" step does not actually stop the workflow; it only exits that step. Subsequent steps still run even when `changed` is false, which can cause `cat ./temp/sponsors.md` to fail (or open a no-op PR). Gate the remaining steps with an `if: steps.sponsors-requires-update.outputs.changed == 'true'` condition (or move the condition to the job) so the workflow truly stops when no updates are needed.</violation>
</file>

<file name=".github/workflows/run-ci.yml">

<violation number="1" location=".github/workflows/run-ci.yml:25">

<violation number="2" location=".github/workflows/run-ci.yml:49">
P2: This step exits 0 but does not stop the job; without `if:` conditions on later steps, CI still runs even when only non-core files changed.</violation>

P1: `matrix.language` is undefined because the matrix only contains `node-version`. CodeQL init will receive an empty language list and fail.</violation>
```

body:
I've fixed the workflow by adding `if` conditions to gate the remaining steps when no sponsor updates are needed.

**Changes made to `.github/workflows/update-sponsor-block.yml`:**

Added `if: steps.sponsors-requires-update.outputs.changed == 'true'` to:
- **Line 44**: "Read sponsors.md file content" step - prevents `cat ./temp/sponsors.md` from failing when the file doesn't exist
- **Line 51**: "Echo sponsors content" step - skips unnecessary output
- **Line 55**: "Create pull request" step - prevents opening a no-op PR

Now when `changed` is `false`, all three steps will be properly skipped instead of just logging a message and continuing.

PR: https://github.com/axios/axios/pull/7341
author: cubic-dev-ai

body:
## Pull request overview

This PR aims to consolidate and modernize GitHub Actions workflows for CI/CD, automate sponsor block updates, and clean up legacy configuration. However, the PR contains several critical issues that will break key functionality.

**Changes:**
- Added new CI workflow (run-ci.yml) that runs tests across Node 12-24 and includes CodeQL/dependency review
- Added automated sponsor block updater workflow with daily schedule
- Simplified publish workflow to trigger on version tags
- Added release branch creation workflow with version bumping
- Removed 9 legacy workflows and issue templates
- Added mise.toml for Node version management

### Reviewed changes

Copilot reviewed 22 out of 23 changed files in this pull request and generated 11 comments.

<details>
<summary>Show a summary per file</summary>

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| `.github/workflows/run-ci.yml` | New consolidated CI workflow replacing old ci.yml; runs tests, builds, and security scans across multiple Node versions |
| `.github/workflows/update-sponsor-block.yml` | New workflow to automate sponsor block updates on a schedule |
| `.github/workflows/release-branch.yml` | New workflow to create release branches with version bumping and testing |
| `.github/workflows/publish.yml` | Simplified NPM publish workflow, now triggers on tags instead of merged PRs |
| `.github/workflows/ci.yml` | Removed old CI workflow (replaced by run-ci.yml) |
| `.github/workflows/sponsors.yml` | Removed old sponsors workflow (replaced by update-sponsor-block.yml) |
| `.github/workflows/pr.yml` | Removed old release PR workflow (replaced by release-branch.yml) |
| `.github/workflows/stale.yml` | Removed stale issue/PR management workflow |
| `.github/workflows/notify.yml` | Removed notification workflow |
| `.github/workflows/labeler.yml` | Removed PR labeler workflow |
| `.github/workflows/npm-tag.yml` | Removed NPM tag management workflow |
| `.github/workflows/depsreview.yaml` | Removed standalone dependency review (now part of run-ci.yml) |
| `.github/workflows/codeql-analysis.yml` | Removed standalone CodeQL workflow (now part of run-ci.yml) |
| `bin/sponsors.js` | Deleted sponsors script that was supposed to be relocated but replacement is missing |
| `.github/labeler.yml` | Removed labeler configuration file |
| `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/*.yml` | Removed all issue template files |
| `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/config.yml` | Removed issue template configuration |
| `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md` | Updated PR template with clearer instructions and branch reference fix |
| `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE.md` | Updated issue template with branch reference fixes and formatting improvements |
| `mise.toml` | Added mise configuration to standardize on Node 22 locally |
</details>



<details>
<summary>Comments suppressed due to low confidence (1)</summary>

**.github/workflows/ci.yml:1**
* The PR description states "Moved legacy workflows to .github/workflows/old" but the workflows are actually being deleted, not moved. There is no `.github/workflows/old/` directory in the repository. This is a discrepancy between the PR description and the actual code changes. If the intention is to preserve these workflows for reference, they should be moved rather than deleted. If deletion is intentional, the PR description should be updated to reflect this.
</details>
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The new publish workflow is significantly simplified compared to the previous version and is missing several important steps: (1) GitHub release creation, (2) Release notes extraction and inclusion, (3) Build version validation via check-build-version.js, (4) NPM tag resolution, and (5) PR notification after publishing. This means releases will only be published to NPM without corresponding GitHub releases, which could break the project's release process and user expectations.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The CI workflow tests against Node.js 12.x and 14.x, which reached End-of-Life on April 30, 2022 and April 30, 2023 respectively. Testing against EOL versions increases CI time and maintenance burden without providing value, as these versions no longer receive security updates. Consider removing these versions from the matrix unless there's a specific requirement to support them.
```suggestion
        node-version: [16.x, 18.x, 20.x, 22.x, 24.x]
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The workflow references `karma.conf.js` but the actual file is `karma.conf.cjs`. This file pattern will not match the actual Karma configuration file, which may cause the workflow to skip tests when changes to the Karma configuration should trigger a test run.
```suggestion
            karma.conf.cjs
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The npm publish command is missing the `--provenance` and `--access public` flags that were present in the previous workflow. The `--provenance` flag enables npm package provenance which provides additional security and transparency by linking the published package to its source repository and build. The `--access public` flag ensures the package is published as public. Consider adding these flags: `npm publish --provenance --access public`
```suggestion
        run: npm publish --provenance --access public
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The workflow attempts to push the release branch twice - first using `ad-m/github-push-action` (step 57-62) and then using `peter-evans/create-pull-request` which also commits and pushes (step 63-77). The `create-pull-request` action handles both committing and pushing changes to a branch, making the explicit push step redundant and potentially causing conflicts. Remove the explicit push step and let the PR creation action handle branch management.
```suggestion

```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The tag pattern `*.*.*` will match any three-part tag separated by dots, which could inadvertently trigger on non-version tags. The old workflow used a more controlled approach by triggering on merged PRs from the release branch. Consider using a more specific semver pattern like `v*.*.*` or `[0-9]+.[0-9]+.[0-9]+` to ensure only version tags trigger the publish workflow.
```suggestion
      - "v*.*.*"
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The CodeQL analysis steps require `security-events: write` permission to upload results, but the workflow only grants `contents: read`. This will cause the CodeQL analysis to fail with a permission error. Add the required permission to the workflow's permissions block.
```suggestion
  contents: read
  security-events: write
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

The old script was located at `./bin/sponsors.js` which is being deleted, but no replacement script has been added at `./resources/update-sponsors.js`. This will cause the workflow to fail when executed.
```suggestion
        run: node ./bin/sponsors.js
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

## Network data written to file

Write to file system depends on [Untrusted data](1).

body:
**29 issues found** across 242 files

**Note:** This PR contains a large number of files. cubic only reviews up to 75 files per PR, so some files may not have been reviewed.

<details>
<summary>Prompt for AI agents (all issues)</summary>

```text

Check if these issues are valid — if so, understand the root cause of each and fix them.


<file name="lib/core/AxiosError.js">

<violation number="1" location="lib/core/AxiosError.js:6">
P2: The `from` method no longer copies all properties from the original error via `toFlatObject`. While the original error is preserved in `cause`, this is a breaking change - code accessing properties like `error.errno` directly (instead of `error.cause.errno`) will fail. Consider restoring `toFlatObject` call or documenting this as a breaking change.</violation>
</file>

<file name="lib/helpers/combineURLs.js">

<violation number="1" location="lib/helpers/combineURLs.js:13">
P1: Regex regression: `/\/?\/$` only removes at most 2 trailing slashes, whereas the original `/\/+$/` removed all trailing slashes. URLs with 3+ trailing slashes will not be normalized correctly, potentially causing malformed combined URLs.</violation>
</file>

<file name="lib/adapters/http.js">

<violation number="1" location="lib/adapters/http.js:361">
P2: Remove debug `console.warn` from production code. This will log to the console whenever an abort error is emitted, which is inappropriate for a library.</violation>
</file>

<file name=".npmignore">

<violation number="1" location=".npmignore:2">
P1: Unignore the `dist/` directory itself before unignoring its contents; otherwise `dist` remains excluded and the package will ship without built artifacts.</violation>

<violation number="2" location=".npmignore:3">
P1: Unignore the `lib/` directory before its contents so the published package includes `lib` artifacts.</violation>
</file>

<file name="lib/helpers/formDataToJSON.js">

<violation number="1" location="lib/helpers/formDataToJSON.js:45">
P2: Incorrect JSDoc type annotation. The parameter description says "The FormData object to convert to JSON" but the type is annotated as `{string}`. Should be `{FormData}` to match the actual expected type and description.</violation>
</file>

<file name=".github/workflows/release-branch.yml">

<violation number="1" location=".github/workflows/release-branch.yml:46">
P2: `commit-message` uses a GitHub expression (`${{version}}`) instead of the action’s `{{version}}` placeholder. This will not resolve to the bumped version and can fail the workflow with an undefined expression. Use the action’s `{{version}}` template instead.</violation>
</file>

<file name="README.md">

<violation number="1" location="README.md:1653">
P2: The fetch adapter example destructures `data` from a Promise without awaiting it, so the snippet doesn’t work as written. Use `await` (or `.then`) when reading the response.</violation>

<violation number="2" location="README.md:1665">
P2: This example also destructures `data` from a Promise without awaiting it. Add `await` (or show a `.then`) so readers can copy/paste a working snippet.</violation>
</file>

<file name="lib/helpers/formDataToStream.js">

<violation number="1" location="lib/helpers/formDataToStream.js:75">
P2: The error message says "10-70 characters" but the condition actually validates "1-70 characters" (`boundary.length < 1`). This mismatch will confuse users when they see an error for a boundary less than 10 chars that should actually be valid.</violation>
</file>

<file name="lib/core/buildFullPath.js">

<violation number="1" location="lib/core/buildFullPath.js:18">
P2: Use strict equality (`===`) instead of loose equality (`==`) when comparing with `false`. Loose equality causes `0 == false` and `"" == false` to be `true`, which could lead to unexpected URL combining behavior.</violation>
</file>

<file name="examples/postMultipartFormData/index.html">

<violation number="1" location="examples/postMultipartFormData/index.html:514">
P2: Potential XSS vulnerability: `err.message` is rendered using `innerHTML`. If the error message contains malicious HTML (e.g., from a malformed server response), it could execute arbitrary scripts. Use `textContent` instead for untrusted content.</violation>

<violation number="2" location="examples/postMultipartFormData/index.html:520">
P2: Potential XSS vulnerability: `err.message` is rendered using `innerHTML` without sanitization. Use `textContent` or properly escape the error message before inserting into the DOM.</violation>
</file>

<file name="examples/abort-controller/index.html">

<violation number="1" location="examples/abort-controller/index.html:60">
P2: Avoid injecting server response data with innerHTML; it can execute HTML/JS if the message contains markup. Use textContent and set the CSS class separately.</violation>

<violation number="2" location="examples/abort-controller/index.html:111">
P2: Do not render server response content via innerHTML. Use textContent and update classes separately to prevent XSS.</violation>
</file>

<file name="lib/helpers/composeSignals.js">

<violation number="1" location="lib/helpers/composeSignals.js:38">
P1: Race condition: if any input signal is already aborted, the composed signal will never be notified. The `abort` event only fires once, so if a signal was aborted before `addEventListener` is called, the handler will never execute. Check `signal.aborted` after adding the listener.</violation>
</file>

<file name="examples/abort-controller/server.js">

<violation number="1" location="examples/abort-controller/server.js:9">
P3: Use the standard JSON media type (`application/json`) so clients parse the response correctly.</violation>
</file>

<file name="examples/server.js">

<violation number="1" location="examples/server.js:73">
P0: Path validation is broken because this file is already in `examples/` directory. `path.join(__dirname, 'examples')` creates `examples/examples/` which doesn't exist, causing all file requests to fail. Should use `__dirname` directly as the base path.</violation>

<violation number="2" location="examples/server.js:77">
P1: The `startsWith` check for path traversal prevention is vulnerable. A path like `/base/path-evil/file` would pass a check for `/base/path`. Use `safeBasePath + path.sep` to ensure proper directory boundary checking.</violation>
</file>

<file name="lib/core/settle.js">

<violation number="1" location="lib/core/settle.js:12">
P3: The JSDoc now claims this function returns the response object, but `settle` does not return anything (it only calls `resolve`/`reject`). This makes the documentation/typing misleading for consumers.</violation>
</file>

<file name="MIGRATION_GUIDE.md">

<violation number="1" location="MIGRATION_GUIDE.md:411">
P2: The documented default `maxContentLength`/`maxBodyLength` values are inaccurate; axios defaults set both to `-1` (no limit). Update the defaults section so migration guidance matches actual behavior.</violation>
</file>

<file name="lib/helpers/buildURL.js">

<violation number="1" location="lib/helpers/buildURL.js:7">
P2: The JSDoc comment is outdated - it claims the function handles `[` and `]` characters, but those replacements were removed from the implementation. Update the comment to match the actual behavior.</violation>
</file>

<file name="lib/adapters/xhr.js">

<violation number="1" location="lib/adapters/xhr.js:112">
P2: Inconsistent config variable: should use `_config` (resolved config) instead of `config` to match the rest of the adapter.</violation>

<violation number="2" location="lib/adapters/xhr.js:178">
P2: Inconsistent config variable: should use `_config` (resolved config) instead of `config` to match the rest of the adapter.</violation>
</file>

<file name="lib/core/dispatchRequest.js">

<violation number="1" location="lib/core/dispatchRequest.js:46">
P1: Unconditionally setting `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` as the default Content-Type for POST/PUT/PATCH requests is incorrect. This will apply the wrong content-type to requests with raw string data or other payloads where `transformRequest` doesn't explicitly set a content-type. This changes axios's default behavior in an unexpected way - for example, `axios.post('/api', 'plain text')` would now incorrectly have `Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded` instead of no content-type. Consider removing this line or making it conditional based on the actual data type.</violation>
</file>

<file name="lib/core/AxiosHeaders.js">

<violation number="1" location="lib/core/AxiosHeaders.js:277">
P1: Logic bug: This always resets `this[$internals]` to a new object on every call, losing track of previously registered accessors. The inner `=` should be `||` to only initialize when the object doesn't exist.</violation>
</file>

<file name="lib/core/Axios.js">

<violation number="1" location="lib/core/Axios.js:184">
P1: Calling `.call()` on potentially undefined `onRejected` will crash. When interceptors are registered without a rejection handler via `use(fulfilled)`, the `rejected` property is `undefined`. Add a null check before invoking.</violation>

<violation number="2" location="lib/core/Axios.js:199">
P2: Using `.then(fn).catch(handler)` instead of `.then(fn, handler)` changes interceptor error propagation semantics. Errors thrown by fulfilled handlers will now be caught by the paired rejection handler, which differs from the standard axios interceptor behavior.</violation>
</file>

<file name="lib/helpers/ZlibHeaderTransformStream.js">

<violation number="1" location="lib/helpers/ZlibHeaderTransformStream.js:16">
P2: Zlib headers are not limited to CMF 0x78. Treating any other CMF as “missing header” will prepend a default header to valid streams (e.g., CMF 0x58), corrupting decompression. Consider validating the full CMF/FLG per RFC 1950 instead of checking only `chunk[0] !== 0x78`.</violation>
</file>
```

</details>

body:
## Pull request overview

This PR simplifies Axios interceptor promise chaining and adjusts GitHub Actions workflows for release automation, sponsor-block updates, and publishing. The main functional impact is on how interceptor errors propagate and on how the release workflow computes and applies new versions.

**Changes:**
- Simplified async request and response interceptor chaining by passing both resolve and reject handlers directly to `Promise.then`, and removed associated tests whose expectations no longer match the new error-propagation behavior.
- Reworked the `release-branch` workflow to use `npm version` directly (with a custom `auto`/beta handling script), updated the release PR creation step, and removed the separate GitHub release tagging step from the `publish` workflow.
- Upgraded `peter-evans/create-pull-request` to v8 in the release and sponsor-block workflows and kept the sponsor-block automation behavior the same.

### Reviewed changes

Copilot reviewed 5 out of 5 changed files in this pull request and generated 4 comments.

<details>
<summary>Show a summary per file</summary>

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| `lib/core/Axios.js` | Adjusts async interceptor promise chaining to use `then(onFulfilled, onRejected)` for both request and response interceptors, changing how errors and values flow through the chain. |
| `test/specs/interceptors.spec.js` | Removes several interceptor error-handling tests that validated the previous async error-propagation and config-preservation semantics. |
| `.github/workflows/update-sponsor-block.yml` | Updates the sponsor-block workflow to use `peter-evans/create-pull-request@v8` while otherwise preserving its behavior. |
| `.github/workflows/release-branch.yml` | Replaces the external bump-version action with an inline `npm version` script, configures git identity, and updates the release PR creation step (including the target branch and action version). |
| `.github/workflows/publish.yml` | Simplifies the publish workflow by removing the GitHub release-tag creation step, leaving only the NPM publish path. |
</details>
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: This change to the async interceptor chain removes the previous `prevResult`-style fallback, so when a request interceptor throws and its `onRejected` handler does not explicitly return a config/response value, the next step in the chain (including `dispatchRequest`) now receives `undefined`, which drops modifications made by earlier interceptors and differs from the behavior of the synchronous interceptor path. If this behavior change is intentional, it should be clearly documented; otherwise, consider restoring a fallback to the last successful value (or ensuring `onRejected` defaults to passing the prior config/response through) to keep async and sync interceptor error-handling semantics aligned.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
In the `TYPE = "auto"` branch, the command that pipes `npm version` through `grep -Eo 'patch|minor|major'` will never match those strings in the output, so the substitution is empty and `npm version` is invoked with no argument, leaving the package version unchanged; given that `auto` is the default input, this means the workflow will not actually bump the version in the common case. Consider either mapping `auto` explicitly to a concrete bump type (e.g., `patch`) or implementing a different mechanism to infer the desired bump type so the version is reliably updated.
```suggestion
            TYPE=patch
          fi
          if [ "$BETA" = "true" ]; then
            npm version "$TYPE" --preid=beta
          else
            npm version "$TYPE"
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The use of the deprecated `::set-output` workflow command to set `newTag` is discouraged and may eventually stop working; the modern pattern is to write outputs to the `$GITHUB_OUTPUT` file instead. To avoid deprecation warnings and potential future breakage, consider updating this step to use the recommended output mechanism.
```suggestion
          echo "newTag=$(node -p \"require('./package.json').version\")" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
```

body:
## Pull request overview

This pull request updates development and production dependencies to their latest minor/patch versions and removes the `release-it` package along with its configuration. However, the PR has a critical issue: it removes the `release-it` package but leaves all the npm scripts that depend on it, which will break the release workflow.

**Changes:**
- Updated multiple devDependencies including major version changes for commitlint (v17 → v20), and minor/patch updates for Babel, ESLint, Karma, Mocha, and others
- Updated production dependencies `follow-redirects` and `form-data` to newer patch versions
- Removed `release-it`, `@release-it/conventional-changelog` packages and the entire `release-it` configuration block
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The `@commitlint/cli` and `@commitlint/config-conventional` packages are being updated from version 17 to version 20, which is a major version jump spanning three major versions (17 -> 18 -> 19 -> 20). Major version changes typically include breaking changes. Ensure that:
1. Any breaking changes in commitlint v18, v19, and v20 are compatible with the project's commit lint configuration
2. The project's commit message format and rules still work as expected
3. CI/CD pipelines that use commitlint are tested with the new version

The commitlint configuration in package.json appears minimal (only customizing header-max-length), which reduces the risk, but testing is still recommended.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The PR description states "Removed the custom `release-it` configuration block from `package.json`" but doesn't mention that the `release-it` package (line removed at old line 149) and `@release-it/conventional-changelog` plugin (removed from devDependencies) are also being removed. The description should be updated to accurately reflect all changes, or if the packages were intended to be kept, they should be retained in devDependencies while only the configuration block is removed.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The `release-it` package and `@release-it/conventional-changelog` plugin have been removed from devDependencies, but the package.json scripts section still contains multiple commands that depend on `release-it`:
- `release:dry`: "release-it --dry-run --no-npm"
- `release:info`: "release-it --release-version"
- `release:beta:no-npm`: "release-it --preRelease=beta --no-npm"
- `release:beta`: "release-it --preRelease=beta"
- `release:no-npm`: "release-it --no-npm"
- `release`: "release-it"

These scripts will fail when executed since the `release-it` package is no longer installed. Either the package should be retained or these scripts should be removed/updated to use an alternative release tool.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
## Pull request overview

This pull request addresses a Denial of Service vulnerability via prototype pollution by adding guards against dangerous property keys (__proto__, constructor, prototype) in the `mergeConfig` function.

**Changes:**
- Added checks to skip dangerous property keys that could lead to prototype pollution attacks
- Changed property lookup from direct bracket access to `Object.hasOwn()` for safer property checking
- Applied code formatting changes (quote style and multi-line formatting)
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The code style change from single quotes to double quotes for the import statement deviates from the established codebase convention. All other files in lib/core/ use single quotes for imports (see Axios.js, AxiosError.js, AxiosHeaders.js, etc.). This should remain as '../utils.js' with single quotes to maintain consistency.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: Object.hasOwn() is a relatively new method (ES2022) that may not be available in all browsers and Node.js versions that Axios supports. The codebase has an existing utility function utils.hasOwnProperty (defined in lib/utils.js:577) specifically designed to safely check for own properties while avoiding ESLint no-prototype-builtins warnings. Consider using utils.hasOwnProperty(mergeMap, prop) instead for better compatibility, or alternatively use the standard pattern: mergeMap.hasOwnProperty(prop) || mergeMap[prop].
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: While the prototype pollution protection for __proto__, constructor, and prototype is good, it should be noted that the security fix may not be complete. The utils.merge function (used in getMergedValue) does not appear to have similar protections against prototype pollution. When merging nested objects, malicious payloads could still potentially pollute Object.prototype through the merge function. Consider adding similar checks in utils.merge or validating that forEach in utils.js properly handles these dangerous keys.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The prototype pollution fix lacks test coverage. The test file test/specs/core/mergeConfig.spec.js has comprehensive tests for various merge scenarios, but there are no tests to verify that the fix prevents prototype pollution attacks. Consider adding tests similar to the one in test/specs/helpers/formDataToJSON.spec.js (line 51-70) that specifically tests __proto__, constructor, and prototype keys to ensure they don't pollute Object.prototype.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The code style change from single quotes to double quotes deviates from the established codebase convention. All other files in lib/core/ use single quotes for the 'use strict' directive (see Axios.js, AxiosError.js, AxiosHeaders.js, etc.). This should remain as 'use strict' with single quotes to maintain consistency.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

**2 issues found** across 1 file

Confidence score: **2/5**
- The `__proto__` pollution fix in `lib/core/mergeConfig.js` only guards top-level keys, so nested objects (e.g., inside headers) can still reach `utils.merge`, which leaves a concrete security/regression risk.
- Using `Object.hasOwn` in `lib/core/mergeConfig.js` may crash on older supported Node.js versions (e.g., 14), creating runtime failures in those environments.
- Pay close attention to `lib/core/mergeConfig.js` - incomplete nested `__proto__` protection and potential Node.js compatibility issues.


<details>

```text
<file name="lib/core/mergeConfig.js">

<violation number="1" location="lib/core/mergeConfig.js:103">
P1: The fix for `__proto__` pollution is incomplete because it only checks top-level keys. Nested objects containing `__proto__` (e.g., in `headers`) are passed to `utils.merge` (via `mergeDeepProperties`), which is still vulnerable to prototype pollution. This allows attackers to bypass the fix by nesting the payload.

To fully fix this, `utils.merge` in `lib/utils.js` should be updated to skip `__proto__`, `constructor`, and `prototype` keys during deep merges.</violation>

<violation number="2" location="lib/core/mergeConfig.js:109">
P2: `Object.hasOwn` was added in Node.js 16.9.0 and is not supported in older environments that Axios likely supports (e.g., Node.js 14). Using it without a polyfill will cause crashes in those environments. Consider using `Object.prototype.hasOwnProperty.call` for better compatibility.</violation>
</file>
```

</details>
