body:
Thanks for the patch @John-pillo1124.

This is the right idea, but it doesn't match the constraints from #10826, and there are a few things to address before this can go in.

### The fetch adapter changes need to come out:
`lib/adapters/fetch.js` is explicitly out of scope in the issue. Fetch is already async, so awaiting `resolveConfig` saves no jank; it just adds a microtask hop on every fetch request for no benefit. Revert this file entirely.

### The XHR adapter is a near-total re-indent at +197/-179:
The issue is specific: do this without re-indenting the body. Wrapping everything in a `handleConfig` callback throws away git blame on the whole file. The shape we want is roughly:

```js
async function (config) {
  const _config = await resolveConfig(config);
  return new Promise(function dispatchXhrRequest(resolve, reject) {
    // existing body unchanged, _config in closure
  });
}
```

### No tests:
The issue calls this out: we need to mock `window.cookieStore` and verify the async branch actually runs, since asserting via `document.cookie` co-stores with cookieStore and doesn't differentiate. At minimum, I want to see:
  - cookieStore mock present, XHR request goes out with the XSRF header populated from the raw `cookieStore` value (no URI decoding).
  - cookieStore undefined, the existing `document.cookie` path is unchanged.
  - non-standard-env shim's new `readAsync()` returns `Promise.resolve(null)` (Web Worker / React Native fallback).
  - `cookieStore.get` rejection falls through to `this.read(name)` cleanly.

That fourth one matters because the `.catch(() => this.read(name))` in `readAsync` swallows every error type silently. That fallback should be deliberate and covered.

### `resolveConfig` is now polymorphic;
It returns the config synchronously on one branch and a `Promise` of the config on the other, depending on whether `window.cookieStore` is detected at runtime. That is a foot-gun for any future caller.
  - Keep `resolveConfig` synchronous and move the async cookieStore read into the XHR adapter directly, calling `cookies.readAsync(xsrfCookieName)` and setting the header there. This better matches the "XHR-only" framing in the issue.

### A few non-blocking nits while you are in there:
  - `lib/helpers/cookies.js:36` the `typeof document === 'undefined'` guard inside the standard-browser-env branch is dead code. The export is already gated on `platform.hasStandardBrowserEnv`, which means `document` exists. Drop it.
  - Trailing whitespace on several of the new lines (`lib/helpers/cookies.js:36`, `lib/adapters/fetch.js:154`, etc.) will likely fail lint once the full CI matrix runs.
  - The commit subject `fix: XHR adapter/avoid main-thread jank` uses `/` as a scope separator. Conventional commits want `fix(xhr): avoid main-thread jank` or similar. Squash the second fix-up commit into the first as well.
  - I would also like to see a microbenchmark or trace in the PR description showing the actual jank reduction. The original issue cites the WHATWG discussion as motivation, but doesn't quantify. 1000 sequential same-origin XHR requests with XSRF, before vs after a long task, would be enough to justify the change concretely.
author: jasonsaayman

body:
## Comparison between inconvertible types Variable 'proxyAuth' cannot be of type null, but it is compared to an expression of type null. --- The best fix is to simplify the object-type guard so it does not compare proxyAuth to null inside a branch where proxyAuth is already truthy.

In lib/adapters/http.js, in the proxy auth handling block (around line 258), replace:

  • const authIsObject = proxyAuth !== null && typeof proxyAuth === 'object';

with:

  • const authIsObject = typeof proxyAuth === 'object';

This is behavior-preserving because the surrounding if (proxyAuth) { ... } prevents null from reaching that line. No new imports, methods, or definitions are needed.

body:
## Comparison between inconvertible types Variable 'proxyAuth' cannot be of type null, but it is compared to an expression of type null. --- To fix this without changing functionality, replace the explicit null comparison on proxyAuth with a truthiness + object-type check:

  • Current:
    • const authIsObject = proxyAuth !== null && typeof proxyAuth === 'object';
  • Replace with:
    • const authIsObject = !!proxyAuth && typeof proxyAuth === 'object';

Why this works:

  • typeof null === 'object', so a guard is needed.
  • !!proxyAuth excludes both null and undefined (and other falsy non-object values), preserving intended behavior.
  • If proxyAuth is an object, this remains true; if it is a string, false; if nullish, false.

Only one line in lib/adapters/http.js needs to be edited (the block around line 272).

+- Unit tests: `npm run test:vitest:unit`; focused unit test: `npm run test:vitest:unit -- tests/unit/path.test.js`.
+- Browser tests need Playwright installed first (`npx playwright install` locally; CI uses `npx playwright install --with-deps`); run `npm run test:vitest:browser:headless` for CI parity.
+- Smoke/module compatibility suites test the packed package, not the source tree: run `npm run build`, `npm pack`, install the tarball into the relevant `tests/smoke/*` or `tests/module/*` package, then run that suite's npm script.
+- CI order is install -> build -> Playwright install -> unit -> browser headless -> pack -> CJS/ESM module and smoke tests -> Bun/Deno smoke tests.

+- Be wary of `Object.prototype` pollution tests on Node 12/14: setting `Object.prototype.get` (or `set`) before any code that calls `Object.defineProperty` with a value-only descriptor will throw `TypeError: Getter must be a function`, because the descriptor inherits the polluted property. Construct servers/clients first, pre-load any lazy-required Node internals (e.g. `require('dns')`), then apply the pollution.
+
+## Local Workflow Files
+- OpenSpec workflows are present under `openspec/` with OpenCode command docs in `.opencode/command/opsx-*.md`; when asked to work an OpenSpec change, follow those command docs and CLI-provided context files instead of guessing artifact names.

<file context>
@@ -191,7 +204,24 @@ The `xsrfHeaderName` is the name of the header to use as a value for `XSRF` toke
+```
+
+::: warning Cross-origin XSRF and `withCredentials`
+`withCredentials` controls whether cross-site requests include credentials (cookies, HTTP auth). In older axios versions, setting `withCredentials: true` implicitly caused axios to set the XSRF header for cross-origin requests. Newer axios separates these concerns: to allow the XSRF header to be sent for cross-origin requests you must set **both** `withCredentials: true` and `withXSRFToken: true`.
+
+```js
</file context>
```

</details>

```suggestion
`withCredentials` controls whether cross-site requests include credentials (cookies, HTTP auth). `withXSRFToken` controls whether axios sets the XSRF header. For cross-origin requests, set `withXSRFToken: true` to force the header; additionally set `withCredentials: true` only when the request also needs credentials/cookies.
```

**1 issue found** across 3 files

Confidence score: **3/5**
- There is a concrete regression risk in `lib/core/AxiosHeaders.js`: preserving non-Latin1 Unicode header values can reach browser platform APIs that may reject them and throw when requests are sent.

body: Thanks for the PR; it looks good overall. Can you just check the comments out? In addition, can you please add a smoke-level test covering `axios.get(new URL(...), { params }) `end-to-end through the mock adapter, not just `buildURL` in isolation.
author: jasonsaayman

body: Coercion happens after the `!params` early return, so the URL-object case still crashes when there are no params. When a user calls `axios.get(urlObject)` with no params and a `baseURL` set, control flows: `Axios.js` → `buildFullPath(baseURL, urlObject, ...)` → `isAbsoluteURL` correctly returns false because of its `typeof url !== 'string'` guard at `lib/helpers/isAbsoluteURL.js:14` → `combineURLs(baseURL, urlObject)` at `lib/helpers/combineURLs.js:13` calls `relativeURL.replace(/^\/+/, '')`. URL objects don't have `.replace()`, so it crashes there with the same error shape as the original bug. Move the coercion to `Axios.js` (around the `config.url = configOrUrl` assignment at `lib/core/Axios.js:87`, or in `mergeConfig`) so every downstream consumer `(buildFullPath, combineURLs, the adapters' typeof url === 'string' && url.startsWith('data:')` checks at `lib/adapters/fetch.js:202`) sees a string. Otherwise, this fix only patches one of three+ live crash paths.
author: jasonsaayman

body: The "without params" test does not exercise the URL-object support. `buildURL(url, null)` hits the if `(!params)` return url early return at `lib/helpers/buildURL.js:32–34`, which returns the URL instance unchanged. The assertion `expect(buildURL(url, null)).toEqual(url.toString())` then compares a URL object to a string and would fail (Vitest's toEqual doesn't equate the two types). If the fix is moved to the right layer per blocker 1. Replace this test with a higher-level one that calls into `axios()` / a dispatchRequest-shaped path with a URL object and a baseURL, since that's the path that's actually broken.

body: `delete redirectOptions.headers[header]` only deletes the lower-case key from `sensitiveHeaders.map(...)`. Axios/follow-redirects header objects can preserve user casing, such as `X-API-Key`, so the PR’s primary example will still leak. Delete by iterating `Object.keys(redirectOptions.headers)` and comparing `name.toLowerCase()` against the sensitive set.

body:
**7 issues found** across 8 files

Confidence score: **2/5**
- There is a high-confidence runtime risk in `package.json`: declaring `undici` as optional conflicts with static imports in the adapter path, which can trigger module resolution failures in Node.js at runtime.
- `lib/adapters/undici.js` has concrete behavior regressions: basic auth URLs can fail with undici `Request`, and the mixed static/dynamic loading plus per-request `new Agent()` adds both correctness and performance risk.
- Test reliability is also shaky in `tests/unit/adapters/undici.test.js` because undefined helper references can cause `ReferenceError` before adapter logic is exercised, reducing confidence in coverage of these changes.
- Pay close attention to `package.json`, `lib/adapters/undici.js`, `tests/unit/adapters/undici.test.js` - runtime dependency resolution, auth handling, and test validity need fixes before merge.


<details>
<summary>Prompt for AI agents (unresolved issues)</summary>

```text

Check if these issues are valid — if so, understand the root cause of each and fix them. If appropriate, use sub-agents to investigate and fix each issue separately.


<file name="lib/adapters/undici.js">

<violation number="1" location="lib/adapters/undici.js:2">
P2: Custom agent: **Flag AI Slop and Fabricated Changes**

Contradictory static and dynamic `undici` loading creates impossible fallback logic. The file static-imports `{ Agent, interceptors } from 'undici'` at module load time, so the module will already fail to load if `undici` is unavailable. Despite this, a runtime `try/catch` around `await import('undici')` assigns `undici = null` on failure, but the code then unconditionally accesses `undici.fetch`, `undici.request`, `undici.Request`, and `undici.Response` — making the null fallback non-functional and unreachable. This is unnecessary defensive handling for an impossible edge case.</violation>

<violation number="2" location="lib/adapters/undici.js:33">
P2: Per-request `new Agent()` causes avoidable connection pool churn and resource overhead</violation>

<violation number="3" location="lib/adapters/undici.js:105">
P1: Basic auth fails because undici `Request` rejects URLs containing credentials. The adapter should sanitize URL credentials before request construction.</violation>
</file>

<file name="tests/unit/adapters/undici.test.js">

<violation number="1" location="tests/unit/adapters/undici.test.js:589">
P2: Abort-normalization test references helpers that are not defined or imported in this file, so it will fail with ReferenceError before exercising the adapter.</violation>

<violation number="2" location="tests/unit/adapters/undici.test.js:686">
P2: Custom agent: **Flag AI Slop and Fabricated Changes**

The test claims to validate AxiosError/ERR_NETWORK handling, but it only checks the nested cause code and never verifies the Axios error shape it names.</violation>
</file>

<file name="package.json">

<violation number="1" location="package.json:53">
P2: Missing `exports` entry for new undici adapter subpath</violation>

<violation number="2" location="package.json:170">
P0: Optional peer dependency `undici` will cause runtime module resolution failures in Node.js because `lib/adapters/undici.js` statically imports `undici` at the top level, and `lib/adapters/adapters.js` statically imports the undici adapter module. This means any Node.js user importing axios without `undici` installed will get `Cannot find module 'undici'` at startup.</violation>
</file>
```

</details>

This should be probbably be handled at adapter level to omit that information and pass it as request initialization options.

- Pay close attention to `lib/adapters/adapters.js` - ensure `undici` follows the same lazy resolver pattern as `fetch` so fallback semantics remain intact.

body:
This is actually tested wrong in`fetch` adapter test suite: https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/v1.x/tests/unit/adapters/fetch.test.js#L468

It uses default Axios instead instead of Fetch adapter instance so it always passes.

I went ahead and created [PR](https://github.com/axios/axios/pull/10896) with adjusted test and new tests based on `http` adapter and URL parsing for `fetch` adapter case. This should also fix Undici test.
author: niksy

body:
Since Undici adapter has different behavior based on passed config options (fast `undici.request` for most use cases, `undici.fetch` for stream/`FormData`, …) we pass currently resolved config to custom fetch adapter getter.

We have that config available in adapter, but that also means we create additional fetch adapter seed cache map entries for each Axios request which seems unecessary and uses more memory just because we have different usage semantics which could be inferred in run time of fetch function instead of Fetch adapter getter.

body:
**6 issues found** across 7 files

Confidence score: **2/5**
- There is a high-confidence CI security risk in `.github/workflows/build-stats.yml`: fork PR code can run in a workflow with `pull-requests: write`, creating a realistic privilege-escalation path and making this unsafe to merge as-is.
- There are concrete runtime/regression bugs in stats/scripts paths: `scripts/helpers/helpers.js` rethrows undefined `e` (masking real filesystem failures), and `scripts/build-stat.js` can use a SHA string where a stats object is expected, producing invalid release diffs (`NaN`).
- Reliability issues in `scripts/helpers/npm.js` and `scripts/helpers/github.js` can cause unhandled stream failures/hangs and empty-comment API failures, so automation behavior may be flaky under error conditions.
- Pay close attention to `.github/workflows/build-stats.yml`, `scripts/helpers/helpers.js`, `scripts/build-stat.js`, `scripts/helpers/npm.js`, and `scripts/helpers/github.js` - they contain the highest-impact security and correctness risks.


<details>
<summary>Prompt for AI agents (unresolved issues)</summary>

```text

Check if these issues are valid — if so, understand the root cause of each and fix them. If appropriate, use sub-agents to investigate and fix each issue separately.


<file name="scripts/helpers/helpers.js">

<violation number="1" location="scripts/helpers/helpers.js:30">
P1: Rethrowing `e` in `listFiles` is a variable typo that turns real filesystem errors into `ReferenceError`.</violation>
</file>

<file name="scripts/build-stat.js">

<violation number="1" location="scripts/build-stat.js:235">
P1: `base === 'release'` selects a SHA string instead of a stat object, so release diff values become invalid (`NaN`).</violation>
</file>

<file name=".github/workflows/build-stats.yml">

author: cubic-dev-ai

body: I think we may need a `transitional` property; otherwise, this would be a breaking change we cannot release. If we can address that and then add some very solid testing here that would probably help this land!
author: jasonsaayman

body:
I think this would create a large breaking change. Removing the `isForm` header overlay means `axios.postForm(url, { foo: 'bar' })` no longer sets `Content-Type: multipart/form-data`, so `lib/defaults/index.js:85` no longer converts plain objects to FormData; those calls fall through to JSON serialisation.

Interceptors/custom adapters that rely on the documented shorthand header would stop seeing the preset multipart content type.
author: jasonsaayman

body: This removal is also an issue and will cause breaking changes.
author: jasonsaayman

body: Addressed in commit ab1fd44c8b936267e684747275cbd2bb948cd612 — added `transitional.formDataContentType` (defaults to `true` for backward compat). Existing code is unaffected; users who need the fix (e.g. React Native Android) can set `transitional: { formDataContentType: false }` globally via `axios.defaults` or per-request. The validator allowlist and transitional defaults are both updated. Seven unit tests cover default behaviour, per-request opt-out, global instance opt-out, and precedence rules.
author: mixelburg

body: Addressed in commit ab1fd44c8b936267e684747275cbd2bb948cd612 — same `transitional.formDataContentType` flag covers both helpers; when `false`, neither the header overlay nor the `isForm` flag produces a Content-Type, matching the fix requested.

body: Mostly looking good, let's just fix the potential regression and add the additional test.
author: jasonsaayman

body: The config.socketPath is read directly before the existing own('socketPath') guard. We cannot have that, as it would regress some of our guards against prototype pollution.
author: jasonsaayman

body: The positive regression test is good; after the above fix, add a negative polluted prototype case near the existing socketPath security tests.
author: jasonsaayman

body: Thanks @jasonsaayman. Good catch. Fixed in e23b20b. I hoisted `const socketPath = own('socketPath')` above the URL parse step and used that value for `urlBase`, so we no longer read `config.socketPath` directly before the own() guard. The synthetic base now only kicks in for an own socketPath, so a polluted prototype can no longer influence URL base selection. I also removed the now duplicate `own('socketPath')` read lower down and reused the single value.
author: devareddy05

body: Thanks @jasonsaayman. Added a negative test next to the existing socketPath security tests in e23b20b. It pollutes `Object.prototype.socketPath`, sends a request with no own socketPath, and asserts the request rejects rather than silently routing to the attacker controlled socket, confirming the polluted prototype value is ignored. The full socketPath suite passes (10 passed), including the existing regression case.

body:
Good catch — thanks for flagging the overload inconsistency.

Pushed 52998e2 to address it: the string branch now differentiates originally whitespace input (e.g. '\n', '\r\n', ' ' from a polyfill's getAllResponseHeaders()) from an originally empty ''. The whitespace case still no-ops (the original bug), but headers.set('', value) keeps throwing header name must be a non-empty string, which is now consistent with headers.set({ '': value }).

Added two regression tests in tests/unit/core/AxiosHeaders.test.js:

throws on set("", value)
throws on set({ "": value }) (overload consistency)
plus an explicit headers.set('\n', 'whatever') no-op test.

**1 issue found** across 2 files Confidence score: **3/5** - There is a concrete medium-risk behavior gap in `lib/adapters/http.js`: `isNodeEnvProxyEnabled()` checks `NODE_USE_ENV_PROXY` but not the equivalent `--use-env-proxy` CLI flag. - When the CLI flag is used without the env var, proxy handling can be applied twice, which creates real regression risk for users running Node with flag-based proxy config. - Given the 6/10 severity and 7/10 confidence, this looks fixable but user-impacting enough to merit caution before merge. - Pay close attention to `lib/adapters/http.js` - align proxy-enabled detection across env-var and CLI-flag paths to avoid duplicate handling. body:
**16 issues found** across 11 files Confidence score: **2/5** - Multiple high-confidence, high-severity issues indicate clear merge risk: `.github/workflows/build-stats.yml` can expose write-scoped credentials to PR-executed code and uses an insecure `workflow_run` pattern in a public-repo context. - There are concrete runtime breakages in helper scripts, including an undefined variable rethrow in `scripts/helpers/helpers.js` and invalid/malformed git format commands in `scripts/helpers/git.js`, which can mask real errors and break commit parsing. - `scripts/build-stat.js` appears to mix SHA strings with expected stat-object fields (`size`/`gzip`) for release comparisons, creating a likely regression in diff calculation behavior rather than a minor edge case. - Pay close attention to `.github/workflows/build-stats.yml`, `scripts/helpers/git.js`, `scripts/helpers/helpers.js`, and `scripts/build-stat.js` - security exposure plus command/parsing defects are likely to cause incorrect behavior or failures.
Prompt for AI agents (unresolved issues) ```text Check if these issues are valid — if so, understand the root cause of each and fix them. If appropriate, use sub-agents to investigate and fix each issue separately. P1: `workflow_run` trigger is insecure in public repositories. The downstream workflow runs with write permissions and secret access regardless of what triggered the upstream workflow, including fork PRs from external contributors. An attacker can poison output artifacts via a PR, and this workflow then consumes attacker-controlled content in a privileged context. Consider alternative approaches like `workflow_call` or adding additional safeguards to validate the triggering context. P2: Action `actions/checkout@v4` is not pinned to a commit SHA. Floating tags can be moved by anyone with write access to the action's repository, enabling supply chain attacks. Pin to a full commit hash (e.g., `actions/checkout@ # v4`) for reproducibility and security. P0: Disable persisted Git credentials before running PR code to avoid leaking a write-scoped token. P2: Potential script injection via template expansion. `${{ github.event.workflow_run.pull_requests[0].base.sha }}` is interpolated directly into the `run:` shell script. Best practice is to pass event data through environment variables rather than direct interpolation in `run:` blocks to avoid code injection. Use an `env:` block to assign the value first, then reference it as `$BASE_SHA` in the script. P2: Action `actions/setup-node@v4` is not pinned to a commit SHA. Pin to a full commit hash for supply chain security. P2: Action `actions/cache@v4` is not pinned to a commit SHA. Pin to a full commit hash for supply chain security. P1: `base === 'release'` assigns a SHA string to `baseStat`, but later code expects a stat object (`baseStat.size`/`baseStat.gzip`). This breaks diff calculations for release-based comparisons. P2: The `--template` argument is ignored because `template` is shadowed by a local variable and the script always reads the default template file. P1: Defaulting `getCommitInfo` to `HEAD~1` changes no-arg callers to use the previous commit, causing snapshot/check logic to target the wrong SHA. P1: `git show` format is missing a newline before `%h`, so `date` and `short` are parsed incorrectly and `short` becomes undefined. P1: The `git branch --format` command has an unclosed quote, which makes the command invalid at runtime. P1: The stream pipeline only handles errors on `extract`; upstream stream errors (especially from gunzip) are not safely routed to `reject`, which can cause unhandled stream errors instead of a rejected promise. P1: `FormData.append` is being called with an options object as the 3rd argument, but Web/undici `FormData` expects a filename string there. This can produce an incorrect uploaded filename and ignore the intended content type metadata. P2: Creating `GithubAPI` at import time makes non-GitHub code paths fail fast when env vars are missing. Initialization should be deferred until GitHub operations are actually needed. P1: Custom agent: **Flag AI Slop and Fabricated Changes** `catch(err)` block references undefined variable `e` in rethrow, causing a `ReferenceError` that masks the original error ```
Reply with feedback, questions, or to request a fix.

P1: `workflow_run` trigger is insecure in public repositories. The downstream workflow runs with write permissions and secret access regardless of what triggered the upstream workflow, including fork PRs from external contributors. An attacker can poison output artifacts via a PR, and this workflow then consumes attacker-controlled content in a privileged context. Consider alternative approaches like `workflow_call` or adding additional safeguards to validate the triggering context.

body:
**21 issues found** Confidence score: **2/5** - Several high-confidence, user-impacting regressions are present (sev 7–8), so merge risk is high rather than a minor polish pass. - In `lib/core/dispatchWithRedirects.js`, the redirect rewrite condition uses `||` instead of `&&`, which can incorrectly rewrite 301/302 flows and change `HEAD` requests to `GET`. - `lib/helpers/isSubdomain.js` and `lib/helpers/BufferedStream.js` introduce concrete runtime behavior breaks (valid subdomains rejected; redirect buffering rejecting supported body types), and `tests/e2e/node/redirects.test.js` has a no-op assertion that weakens detection of these failures. - Pay close attention to `lib/core/dispatchWithRedirects.js`, `lib/helpers/isSubdomain.js`, `lib/helpers/BufferedStream.js`, and `index.d.cts` - redirect semantics and type/config correctness are currently fragile.
Prompt for AI agents (unresolved issues) ```text Check if these issues are valid — if so, understand the root cause of each and fix them. If appropriate, use sub-agents to investigate and fix each issue separately. P3: Fallback UTF-8 encoding uses deprecated `unescape(...)`; use the modern percent-decoding pattern for consistency and future compatibility. P2: `useAxiosRedirects` is read from `config.transitional` directly, bypassing own-property checks and weakening prototype-pollution hardening. P2: `AxiosBufferingConfig` has a typo (`timout`) that makes the typed option ineffective at runtime. P1: Custom agent: **Flag AI Slop and Fabricated Changes** `beforeRedirect` type has misplaced parentheses, turning a union of two callback signatures into a single function that returns `void | ((redirectMeta) => false | void)`. The `)` should close the first callback before the `|`, not after the second callback. P2: `list()` incorrectly ignores supported falsy inputs (`0`, `false`) due to a truthiness guard. P2: Malformed `Location` headers can throw raw `TypeError` during URL parsing; wrap parsing and throw `AxiosError` (`ERR_INVALID_URL`) for consistent error handling. P1: Redirect method rewrite condition is logically incorrect (`||` instead of `&&`), so 301/302 always enter rewrite path and can incorrectly change `HEAD` requests to `GET`. P2: The new ERR_STREAM_FLUSHED branch drops axios error context by throwing `AxiosError.from(err.cause)` without `config`/`request`, which can break interceptors/retry logic expecting `error.config`. P2: `useAxiosRedirects` is read via prototype chain; inherited values can unexpectedly enable redirect flow. P1: Constructor validation is too strict: it rejects Request/ReadableStream/blob-like bodies that redirect buffering intentionally supports, causing runtime failure before streaming begins. P1: `toBeDefined` matcher is called as a property access instead of a method call, making the assertion a no-op. P3: Comment typo: 'coping' should be 'copying' P2: `reader.cancel()` in `finally` can mask the original stream read error if cancel also rejects. P2: Abort path does not unsubscribe the signal listener, causing a listener leak on canceled timeouts. P2: `toBase64` uses `String.fromCharCode(...bytes)`, which can fail for large inputs due to spread argument limits. P1: Incorrect subdomain boundary check — `diff > 2` rejects single-character subdomain labels. For example, `isSubdomain('a.example.com', 'example.com')` incorrectly returns `false`. The condition should be `diff > 0` to allow any valid subdomain label of one or more characters plus the dot separator. P2: The `react-native` field is missing the `./lib/core/dispatchWithRedirects.js` → `./lib/helpers/null.js` mapping that was added to the `browser` field. The `browser` and `react-native` sections were previously identical, and the PR description states this feature is not supported in client environments. body: By looking at it `maxRedirects: 0` throws `Too many redirects` instead of returning the `3xx` response for normal `validateStatus` handling, lets preserve existing `maxRedirects: 0` semantics.
author: jasonsaayman

body: Since `beforeRedirect` can mutate `config.url` after we have computed `redirectTo`, bypassing protocol allow-list, downgrade checks, and sanitisation, validate the final dispatch URL after the hook, or make `redirectTo` the only mutable target.
author: jasonsaayman

body: Authorization/Cookie headers are preserved for redirects from a host to any subdomain via `isSubdomain`, which is weaker than the current `follow-redirects` cross-host stripping and can leak credentials to, for example, `evil.api.example.com`. Please strip sensitive headers whenever host/origin changes by default; require an explicit opt-in hook to preserve them.
author: jasonsaayman

body: abort/cancel does not interrupt an in-flight `iterator.next()`, so a stalled upload source can hang cancellation until it yields. Race reads with the signal and cleans up the iterator/source.
author: jasonsaayman

body: `Request`/`ReadableStream` bodies are selected for buffering, but `BufferedStream` rejects non-async-iterables at `lib/helpers/BufferedStream.js:18`, let's align accepted body types or narrow the buffering functionality.
author: jasonsaayman

body: Can we move the updates to the pre-release docs and pre-release changelog? Keeps the repo and docs as the real SOT.
author: jasonsaayman

body: Prototype pollution can silently pass every request into the experimental redirect path via inherited transitional.useAxiosRedirects. This defeats the “opt-in only” safety boundary.
author: jasonsaayman

body: Redirect options are destructured from normal objects, so inherited `allowDowngrade`, `maxRedirects`, `buffering`, `beforeRedirect`, and related fields can drive security behaviour. This would reopen the read-side prototype-pollution gadget class from the 2026 advisories.
author: jasonsaayman

body: Part of issue mentioned in LN24
author: jasonsaayman

body: Part of issue mentioned in LN24
author: jasonsaayman

body: `sanitize()` clears `redirectedUrl.username/password` but does not write the sanitized URL back to `redirectedConfig.url`. A relative redirect from a URL containing `userinfo` can still regenerate `Basic auth` in the next dispatch. After clearing, we should always assign.
author: jasonsaayman

body: `beforeRedirect` can mutate config.url after protocol, downgrade, and sanitizer inputs are computed. The actual dispatched URL can differ from the checked URL.
author: jasonsaayman

body: Streamed upload `maxBodyLength` is only enforced for `config.maxRedirects === 0`. The new `useAxiosRedirects` native transport path and HTTP/2 path can send past `maxBodyLength`, regressing the streamed-upload limit advisories we have received in the past.
author: jasonsaayman

body: Redirect responses are buffered before following. A malicious server can send `302 Location: /next` and then stream an unbounded body forever, causing memory growth or hang before axios follows.
author: jasonsaayman

**3 issues found** across 29 files Confidence score: **3/5** - In `lib/adapters/fetch.js`, the stream `maxBodyLength` wrapping path ignores `supportsRequestStream`, so environments without request stream support can still be handed a `ReadableStream`, leading to request failures/regressions for streamed uploads—gate that wrapping logic on `supportsRequestStream` before merging. - In `lib/utils.js` (`isSafeIterable`), reading `thing[Symbol.iterator]` before pollution checks can still trigger polluted `Object.prototype` accessors, which may throw or execute unintended code paths at runtime—move the safety checks ahead of property access (or use a safe lookup pattern) to de-risk this. - In `lib/helpers/shouldBypassProxy.js` (`isIPv6Unspecified`), not recognizing compressed all-zero IPv6 forms can make equivalent unspecified addresses bypass proxy rules inconsistently, causing surprising networking behavior—normalize/expand IPv6 unspecified matching and add coverage for compressed variants. Reply with feedback, questions, or to request a fix.

body:
The bracket alternative can scan to the end from each unmatched `[` and causes quadratic behaviour on malformed field names - replace with a linear tokeniser or a fail-fast grammar, and add regression coverage for long malformed bracket input.

The new grammar also allows `.` and `[` inside bracket-captured segments, while the new comment says path segments exclude `., [,` and `]` - define the intended grammar and cover cases like `foo[bar.baz]`.

body: The full normalised URL is embedded in the error message, which is always included by AxiosError.toJSON() and is commonly logged directly. This can expose credentials or query tokens in a field that Axios’s existing config redaction cannot clean. Please sanitise/redact sensitive URL parts or otherwise make this respect the existing redaction model, and cover that behaviour with a focused test.

P1: Shorthand IPv4 normalization for 2/3-part hosts does not match Node's WHATWG URL parser, causing NO_PROXY parser differentials for tails that are multi-byte integers or use hex/octal.

<details>
<summary>Prompt for AI agents</summary>

```text
Check if this issue is valid — if so, understand the root cause and fix it. At lib/helpers/shouldBypassProxy.js, line 97:

<comment>Shorthand IPv4 normalization for 2/3-part hosts does not match Node's WHATWG URL parser, causing NO_PROXY parser differentials for tails that are multi-byte integers or use hex/octal.</comment>

<file context>
@@ -7,6 +7,108 @@ const isIPv4Loopback = (host) => {
+  // Tail must be 1..tailSlots decimal digits — Node's shorthand expansion only
+  // accepts decimal digits in the tail (no octal/hex), and accepting more here
+  // would diverge from Node for the common case.
+  if (!/^[0-9]+$/.test(tail) || tail.length < 1 || tail.length > tailSlots) {
+    return host;
+  }
</file context>
```

</details>

```suggestion
+  // Tail is parsed as a full IPv4 number (hex/octal/decimal) and then packed
+  // into the remaining octets from the right, matching Node's URL parser.
+  const tailValue = parseIPv4Octet(tail);
+  if (tailValue === null) return host;
+  const maxTail = (1 << (8 * tailSlots)) - 1;
+  if (tailValue < 0 || tailValue > maxTail) return host;
+
+  const tailOctets = new Array(tailSlots).fill(0);
+  for (let i = tailSlots - 1, v = tailValue; i >= 0; i--, v >>= 8) {
+    tailOctets[i] = v & 0xff;
+  }
```

 - **HTTP Adapter - native env proxy:** Avoid double-applying environment proxy handling when Node.js native HTTP proxy support is active for the selected agent. Axios still resolves env proxies itself when the selected agent is not using Node's `proxyEnv` support. (**#10942**, closes **#7299**)
 - **HTTP Adapter - socketPath:** Path-only request URLs (e.g. `'/foo'`) now work again with `config.socketPath`, fixing the `TypeError [ERR_INVALID_URL]` regression introduced in 1.7.4 when `new URL()` was added to the dispatch path. A synthetic `http://localhost` base is supplied only when an own `socketPath` is set, so absolute URLs, non-socket requests, and prototype-polluted `socketPath` values are unaffected. (**#6611**)
-- **NO_PROXY IPv4 normalization:** `shouldBypassProxy` now canonicalises NO_PROXY entries and request hostnames written in Node's URL-parser-accepted shorthand, octal (`0177.0.0.1`), and hex (`0x7f.0.0.1`) forms to dotted-decimal before comparison, so a `NO_PROXY=127.1` entry matches a request to `http://127.0.0.1/` (and vice versa). The shorthand tail of a 2- or 3-part input is parsed as a full IPv4 number (hex/octal/decimal) and packed low-byte-right into the remaining octets, matching Node's URL parser, so `NO_PROXY=127.65535` matches `http://127.0.255.255/`, `NO_PROXY=127.0x00ff` matches `http://127.0.0.255/`, and `NO_PROXY=127.0177` matches `http://127.0.0.127/`. The helper is fail-safe: invalid octets (out of 0-255, non-hex digits), 1-part inputs, 5+ parts, and out-of-range tails (e.g. `127.16777216`) are returned unchanged so the policy falls through to non-bypass. (**#11029**)

body:
**2 issues found** across 5 files Confidence score: **3/5** - In `lib/core/methodList.js`, omitting `purge`, `link`, and `unlink` from the shared method list while Axios types/interfaces support them can cause runtime/type drift in method-based header/default handling, so those methods may behave inconsistently after merge — add the missing methods to keep runtime behavior aligned with the declared Axios method surface before merging. - In `lib/adapters/http.js`, the new httpVersion `AxiosError` paths drop the request `config`, which makes these failures harder to diagnose and less consistent with other adapter errors; merging as-is risks poorer debugging and error handling in production — include `config` on these errors to match existing AxiosError conventions before merging. Reply with feedback, questions, or to request a fix.
